sheets.title: Associate in Applied Science - Artificial Intelligence
sheets.overview:
Artificial intelligence is transforming every industry—and this program puts you at the heart of it. You’ll learn to build intelligent systems that make predictions, recognize images, understand language, and learn from data, using the same Python tools and machine-learning techniques powering today’s most innovative companies.

Through hands-on courses in machine learning, deep learning, chatbots, and computer vision—grounded in applied math, statistics, and ethics—you’ll move from your first Python program to training and deploying real AI models, building portfolio-ready skills with no prior experience required. The AAS in Artificial Intelligence degree readies you for one of technology’s fastest-growing fields.
sheets.employmentOutlook:
The opportunities in AI are exploding. The U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics projects employment for data professionals to grow at least 38% by 2030, and in the Charlotte region—a major financial and technology hub—employers across banking, healthcare, and tech are investing heavily in AI talent, driving strong demand and competitive salaries for graduates who can build and maintain these systems.

Your degree can open doors to roles like AI Engineer, Machine Learning Engineer, Data Scientist, Computer Vision Engineer, NLP Engineer, and AI Research Scientist.

(Please note: CPCC does not guarantee employment)

planOfStudy.note: You may have completed program certificate C25710-C1. Confirm eligibility with your academic advisor.

sheets.title: Associate in Applied Science - Software Engineering
sheets.overview:
Software powers everything around us—and software engineers are the people who build it. In this program you’ll learn to design, develop, test, and deploy real applications while mastering programming logic, databases, web development, and project management.

The Application Development track sharpens your skills in Java and enterprise programming—from data structures and algorithms to server-side development and emerging technologies—so you can build robust, scalable applications that businesses depend on.
sheets.employmentOutlook:
Demand for software talent keeps climbing. The U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics projects software developer employment to grow much faster than average through the decade, with strong salaries across nearly every industry. Application developers who can build and maintain dependable software are especially sought after.

Your degree can open doors to roles like Java Developer, Application Developer, Software Engineer, Back-End Developer, and Systems Programmer.

(Please note: CPCC does not guarantee employment)

Full-stack developers who can work across the entire web stack are in especially high demand.

Your degree can open doors to roles like Full-Stack Developer, Web Developer, Front-End Developer, Back-End Developer, and Web Application Programmer.

sheets.title: Associate in Applied Science - Data Analysis
sheets.overview:
Data is the language of modern business—and data analysts are the people who turn it into decisions. In this program you’ll learn to collect, clean, analyze, and visualize data using today’s essential tools: Python, databases, spreadsheets, statistics, and analytics platforms.

The Data Analysis track deepens your statistical and programming skills—spreadsheet analysis, data-science programming, SAS, and data warehousing and mining—so you can dig into large datasets and surface the patterns that drive smart decisions.
sheets.employmentOutlook:
Organizations everywhere need people who can make sense of their data. The U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics projects data and analytics roles to grow much faster than average through the decade, with strong salaries across nearly every industry for analysts who can find the story in the numbers.

Your degree can open doors to roles like Data Analyst, Statistical Analyst, Data Science Associate, Database Analyst, and Data Warehouse Analyst.

(Please note: CPCC does not guarantee employment)

The Data Visualization track teaches you to transform complex data into clear, compelling dashboards and visuals using modern analytics and visualization tools—plus SAS and data warehousing—so decision-makers can instantly see the story in the numbers.

sheets.title: Associate in Applied Science - Cybersecurity
sheets.overview:
Cybersecurity professionals protect the systems, networks, and data that organizations depend on—and demand for them in the Charlotte region has never been higher. You’ll build a strong security foundation in networking, operating systems, Linux, virtualization, protocol analysis, and security concepts, then specialize in a career track.

The Digital Forensics track teaches you to investigate cybercrime, recover data, and analyze digital evidence—the incident-response and investigation skills that financial, healthcare, and government employers rely on, aligned with industry digital-forensics certifications.
sheets.employmentOutlook:
Charlotte’s financial institutions, healthcare systems, and government agencies need professionals who can investigate breaches, collect evidence, and respond to incidents—part of a regional market of 17,000+ cybersecurity openings averaging nearly $125,000.

Your degree can open doors to roles like Digital Forensics Investigator, Incident Responder, Cybercrime Analyst, and Forensic Analyst.

(Please note: CPCC does not guarantee employment)

sheets.title: Associate in Applied Science - Cybersecurity
sheets.concentration: Red Team Career Track (Ethical Hacking & Pen Testing)
sheets.overview:
Cybersecurity professionals protect the systems, networks, and data that organizations depend on—and demand for them in the Charlotte region has never been higher. You’ll build a strong security foundation in networking, operating systems, Linux, virtualization, protocol analysis, and security concepts, then specialize in a career track.

The Red Team track trains you to think like an attacker—perimeter defense, ethical hacking, and Python-powered exploitation—so you can find and fix vulnerabilities before adversaries do. Coursework aligns with Certified Ethical Hacker (CEH) and PenTest+.
sheets.employmentOutlook:
Organizations need people who think like attackers. Charlotte-area employers have posted 1,525 Cybersecurity Engineer and 798 Application Security Engineer roles calling for offensive-security skills, often with entry salaries of $60,000–$70,000—within a regional market of 17,000+ openings averaging nearly $125,000.

Your degree can open doors to roles like Penetration Tester, Ethical Hacker, Cybersecurity Engineer, Application Security Engineer, and Vulnerability Analyst.

(Please note: CPCC does not guarantee employment)

sheets.title: Associate in Applied Science - Cybersecurity
sheets.concentration: Blue Team Career Track (Network Defense & Security)
sheets.overview:
Cybersecurity professionals protect the systems, networks, and data that organizations depend on—and demand for them in the Charlotte region has never been higher. You’ll build a strong security foundation in networking, operating systems, Linux, virtualization, protocol analysis, and security concepts, then specialize in a career track.

The Blue Team track focuses on defending networks in real time—intrusion detection, network programmability, and endpoint detection and response. Coursework aligns with SSCP and Cisco CyberOps Associate certifications.
sheets.employmentOutlook:
Defenders keep organizations running. Over recent years, Charlotte-area employers posted 3,880 Information Security Engineer and 2,842 Information Security Analyst roles—the threat-detection and defense skills this pathway builds—within a regional market of 17,000+ cybersecurity openings averaging nearly $125,000.

Your degree can open doors to roles like Information Security Analyst, SOC Analyst, Intrusion Detection Specialist, Endpoint Security Engineer, and Network Defender.

(Please note: CPCC does not guarantee employment)

sheets.title: Associate in Applied Science - IT Technical Support
sheets.overview:
Every organization runs on technology—and IT technical support professionals are the people who keep it running. In this hands-on program you’ll learn to set up, secure, troubleshoot, and support computers, networks, Windows and Linux systems, virtualization, and cloud services.

You’ll also build the user-support and professional skills that make great support teams, graduating ready to be the person coworkers and customers count on when it matters most.
sheets.employmentOutlook:
Skilled IT support is in constant demand. The U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics projects steady growth for computer support specialists, and nearly every employer—from banks and hospitals to schools and small businesses—needs people who can keep systems running and users productive. It’s also a proven launchpad into networking, cybersecurity, and systems administration.

Your degree can open doors to roles like Help Desk Technician, Desktop Support Specialist, IT Support Technician, Systems Support Analyst, and Technical Support Engineer.

(Please note: CPCC does not guarantee employment)

sheets.title: Associate in Applied Science - Cloud and Networking Technologies
sheets.overview:
Networking and the cloud now power everything—and the two have become inseparable. This program gives you the integrated skill set modern IT runs on: enterprise networking, switching and routing, Linux and Windows administration, virtualization, cloud storage, and data center management.

Through hands-on labs you’ll configure real networks, stand up servers, and manage cloud and virtualized infrastructure—graduating ready to keep an organization’s systems fast, secure, and always available. This degree unifies Central Piedmont’s Network Management and Cloud & Virtualization programs into one comprehensive, in-demand credential.
sheets.employmentOutlook:
The Charlotte region is hungry for networking and cloud talent. Recent analytics show 98,293 unique IT openings across 7,405 employers—including 3,414 Systems Administrator, 1,484 Cloud Engineer, and 1,254 Infrastructure Engineer roles—with a median advertised salary of $109,952 for computer and mathematical occupations.

The skills you’ll build are exactly what employers list: Linux administration appears in 9,900+ local postings, Microsoft Azure in 9,173, and network routing in 4,388. Your degree can open doors to roles like Network Administrator, Cloud Engineer, Systems Administrator, Infrastructure Engineer, and Network Technician.

(Please note: CPCC does not guarantee employment)

sheets.title: Associate in Applied Science - Simulation and Game Development

planOfStudy.note: You may have completed program certificate C25450-21. Confirm eligibility with your academic advisor.

rows.name: Introduction to Simulation and Game Development
Simulation and Game Development Design II
Simulation and Game Development Capstone Project

planOfStudy.note: You may have completed a program certificate(s). Confirm eligibility with your academic advisor.

sheets.overview:
Turn your passion for games into a career. Central Piedmont is home to the first state-approved game-development degree in the country, and this program immerses you in the full craft—design, programming, and 3D modeling and animation—using the same industry-standard tools studios rely on, from Unreal Engine and Unity to Maya, ZBrush, and the Adobe suite.

The 3D Modeling & Animation concentration builds the art skills studios hire for—modeling, texturing, rigging, character development, and 2D/3D animation—so you graduate with a portfolio of game-ready assets.
sheets.employmentOutlook:
The game industry is booming, and the skills you’ll build reach well beyond it. Graduates work in entertainment, education, healthcare, engineering, and simulation—anywhere interactive 3D and software come together—and hands-on, portfolio-driven coursework plus work-based learning get you ready for real studio work.

Your degree can open doors to roles like 3D Modeler, Character Artist, Technical Artist, Animator, and Game Artist.

(Please note: CPCC does not guarantee employment)
